did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning and deep learning methods for the automatic assessment of Actinic Keratosis (AK) stage. Specifically, it employed a CFPNet-M model for automated segmentation of high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) data, extracted convolutional neural network features (using models like EfficientNet, MobileNet, VGG-16, DenseNet-201) and handcrafted features from dermatoscopic images, and utilized a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification. The study achieved an accuracy of 81% for dermatoscopic analysis with neural networks and 79% when combined with ultrasound scans.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is titled 'Automatic Assessment of AK Stage Based on Dermatoscopic and HFUS Imaging—A Preliminary Study' and presents original experimental work. It describes a prospective clinical study involving 54 patients, details the development and training of machine learning models (CFPNet-M, SVM, neural networks) on specific datasets, and reports new results including classification accuracy (81% for dermatoscopic analysis with neural networks, 79% combined with ultrasound). It includes Methods, Results, and Conclusions sections typical of primary research.

title: Automatic Assessment of AK Stage Based on Dermatoscopic and HFUS Imaging—A Preliminary Study
brief_summary: This study evaluated the utility of machine learning algorithms for automatically staging Actinic Keratosis (AK) using multimodal dermatoscopic and high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) images. The research involved a prospective clinical study of 54 patients, generating a dataset of 162 image pairs analyzed via the Zalaudek scale. The proposed framework utilized a CFPNet-M model for automated HFUS segmentation and combined handcrafted ultrasound features with convolutional neural network features (from models like EfficientNet) extracted from dermatoscopic images. These features were classified using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) to determine the AK stage. The most promising results were achieved with dermatoscopic analysis using neural networks (81% accuracy) and its combination with ultrasound scans (79% accuracy). The authors conclude that while promising, further experiments with larger datasets are needed to improve classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the study. The paper focuses on the automatic assessment of the stage (grade 1, 2, or 3) of Actinic Keratosis using dermatoscopic and high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) imaging. The dataset consisted of 162 image pairs from 54 patients with AK lesions.
Mentioned in the introduction as part of a binary classification task (AK vs. NAK) discussed in a cited reference [55], but not the target of the current study's experiments.

Mentioned in the introduction as a potential progression outcome of AK (rate of progression estimated at 0.1–20%) and in the context of HFUS assessment difficulties, but not studied as a target class in the experiments.
Mentioned in the introduction regarding the utility of dermatoscopy and HFUS in diagnosing malignant lesions, but not a target class in this study.
Mentioned in the introduction regarding the application of HFUS in dermato-oncology, but not a target class in this study.
Mentioned in the introduction as a category of skin cancer diagnosed via dermatoscopy, but not a specific target class in this study.

all_task_types.details: The study performs multiclass classification to distinguish between three stages of Actinic Keratosis based on dermatoscopic and high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) imaging features. The primary classifier used is a Support Vector Machine (SVM) with a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel. The task involves predicting discrete diagnostic categories (AK stages) rather than continuous values. Additionally, a CFPNet-M model was used for segmentation (a secondary task) to extract features, but the final assessment task is classification.

all_task_types.target: Skin layers in HFUS images (entry echo layer and SLEB layer)
all_task_types.details: A CFPNet-M model was used for automated segmentation of high-frequency ultrasound data to identify specific skin layers (entry echo and subepidermal low echogenic band) for feature extraction. This is a preprocessing step for the primary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Prospective Clinical Cohort (Poznan University of Medical Sciences)
datasets_used.description: A prospective clinical study dataset collected from 54 patients presenting with Actinic Keratosis (AK) lesions at the outpatient clinic of the Department of Dermatology, Poznan University of Medical Sciences, between June 2023 and April 2024. The dataset consists of 162 image pairs, where each pair includes a dermatoscopic image (acquired with DermLite DL5 coupled to an iPhone 8) and a high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) image (acquired with DermaScan C, 20 MHz).

use.training: The network models (CFPNet-M, EfficientNet, etc.) were trained on public datasets, not this specific cohort. This cohort was used for feature extraction and final classification evaluation.
use.testing: The 162 image pairs from the 54 patients were used to evaluate the classification performance of the Support Vector Machine (SVM) using features extracted from dermatoscopic and HFUS images.
use.other: The CFPNet-M model used for HFUS segmentation was trained on public datasets. The dermatoscopic and HFUS images from this cohort were used to extract handcrafted features and CNN features for the final multimodal classification task.

datasets_used.name: Public Datasets (Unspecified)
datasets_used.description: The paper states that 'The network models were trained on public datasets' but does not specify the names, sources, or contents of these public datasets in the provided text.

use.training: Used to train the CFPNet-M model for HFUS segmentation and potentially the convolutional neural networks (EfficientNet, MobileNet, VGG-16, DenseNet-201) for feature extraction.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure robustness to outliers in the data before feature ranking and classification.
To train and validate the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier, with each patient in a separate fold.
To handle class imbalance by generating synthetic data for minority classes in each training set.
To evaluate the accuracy of the automated segmentation of HFUS images (entry echo layer and SLEB layer).
To assess the agreement of the classification results, interpreted using standard rules (e.g., 0.81–1.00 almost perfect agreement).
To determine whether significant differences exist between groups (AK stages) for interpretable features.
To calculate the effect size of the differences found by the Kruskal–Wallis test.

statistical_methods_used.method: Dunn’s post hoc test with Bonferroni correction
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine specific differences between groups after finding significant differences with the Kruskal–Wallis test.
To determine statistical significance for the Kruskal–Wallis test and post hoc comparisons.

hypothesis_testing.test: Kruskal–Wallis test with Dunn’s post hoc test and Bonferroni correction
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There are no significant differences in the median values of the interpretable features between the different AK stages (groups).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There are significant differences in the median values of the interpretable features between at least two of the AK stages.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The Kruskal–Wallis test was used to determine whether significant differences exist between groups for interpretable features. Significant differences were found for epidermal characteristics related to thickness and structure, with post hoc analysis showing differences between groups 1 and 3. For texture features of the SLEB layer, significant differences were noted for MEP and pixel intensity distribution. For combined layers, significant differences were found for homogeneity and GLCM correlation between group 1 and the others. Effect sizes were calculated using eta squared.

Prospective Clinical Cohort (Poznan Univ.description: Dataset of 162 image pairs from 54 patients with Actinic Keratosis (AK) lesions. Classification performed using SVM with leave-one-out validation and SMOTE for class imbalance.
Prospective Clinical Cohort (Poznan Univ.notes: The most promising results were obtained for dermatoscopic analysis with neural network model (accuracy 81%) and its combination with ultrasound scans (accuracy 79%). The classification results for manually segmented regions were the same as for automated segmented regions. Statistical analysis using Kruskal-Wallis test showed significant differences in median values of epidermal characteristics related to thickness and structure between AK stages.

architecture_used.main_model: Multimodal framework combining CFPNet-M for segmentation, EfficientNet (and other CNNs like MobileNet, VGG-16, DenseNet-201) for feature extraction, and Support Vector Machine (SVM) for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: The network models were trained on public datasets (specific names not reported). CFPNet-M was fine-tuned. EfficientNet was used for feature extraction from dermatoscopic images.

CFPNet_M.type: U-shaped architecture with contextual feature pyramid module
CFPNet_M.description: Utilizes a contextual feature pyramid module consisting of multiple convolutional layers with different coefficients to capture features on different perceptual fields. Outputs are combined and processed downstream to pick local and global context information.

EfficientNet.usage: Feature extraction from dermatoscopic images. Features from the last fully connected layer were used.

CFPNet_M.augmentation: Geometric augmentation including vertical shifts, 10-degree rotations, and vertical axis reflection

SVM.validation_method: Leave-one-out validation (each patient in a separate fold)

classification_or_output_head.SVM: Support Vector Machine with RBF kernel used for final AK stage assessment (classes 1, 2, 3).

preprocessing_techniques: Automated segmentation of HFUS skin layers (entry echo and SLEB) using CFPNet-M model
Removal of round corner frames and measurement marks from dermatoscopic images using image intensity adjustment, thresholding, and fuzzy c-means clustering

Hair removal from dermatoscopic images using the modified DullRazor algorithm (tested on grey image and R, G, B channels; best results on red channel with vector kernel size 7-21 pixels, 45-degree rotation) and top hat transform for white hair detection
Morphological opening to remove noise from hair masks
In-painting technique with structural element size selected according to hair thickness to smooth images after hair removal
Decomposition of dermatoscopic images into red, green, blue, and hue, saturation, and value channels
Z-score normalization of features to ensure robustness to outliers
Feature ranking using minimum redundancy and maximum relevance (MRMR) algorithm
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) for handling class imbalance in training sets
Geometric augmentation for CFPNet-M training (vertical shifts, 10-degree rotations, vertical axis reflection)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during the training of the CFPNet-M model for HFUS segmentation. Techniques included vertical shifts, 10-degree rotations, and vertical axis reflection.

Used for handling class imbalance in the SVM classification task. Synthetic data was generated for minority classes in each training set to reinforce existing information without creating new signals.
Applied during the training of the EfficientNet model for dermatoscopic feature extraction to reduce class imbalance.

conclusions: The application of machine learning-based algorithms in dermatoscopic and ultrasound image analysis may be beneficial in clinical practice for predicting the risk of progression of Actinic Keratosis (AK).
The most promising classification results were achieved using dermatoscopic analysis with a neural network model (81% accuracy) and its combination with ultrasound scans (79% accuracy).

Automated segmentation of high-frequency ultrasound (HFUS) data using the CFPNet-M model ensures that the segmentation step does not negatively influence further analysis, as classification results were consistent with those obtained from manually segmented regions.
Further experiments with larger datasets are warranted to improve the classification accuracy of the system.
Histopathological examination is needed in future studies to correlate HFUS scans with findings seen throughout the skin layers in AK and field cancerization.
important_remarks: The study is a preliminary investigation with a small sample size (54 patients, 162 image pairs), limiting statistical power and generalizability.
The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, necessitating the use of SMOTE for minority classes, which may introduce synthetic bias.
Network models (CFPNet-M, EfficientNet) were trained on unspecified public datasets, not the study's own cohort, raising potential domain shift concerns.
The study population is highly biased towards elderly patients (median age 74) with Fitzpatrick I–II skin types, limiting applicability to other demographics.
Classification accuracy for HFUS handcrafted features was low (48.3%), indicating that current handcrafted ultrasound features are insufficient for distinguishing AK stages.
The study lacks external validation; results are derived from a single center (Poznan University of Medical Sciences) using specific devices (DermLite DL5, DermaScan C).
No histopathological correlation was performed in this study, relying instead on clinico-dermatoscopic classification by a council of experts.
The authors acknowledge that the limited number of training examples prevented the use of deep convolutional models for all steps, relying on SVM instead.
The study is labeled as 'Preliminary' and explicitly calls for larger datasets and further experiments to improve classification accuracy.
